questions.question: Explain why a pull-up resistor is necessary when interfacing a push-button with a microcontroller GPIO pin configured as an input.
expectedPoints: A button typically connects the pin to GND when pressed.
When the button is not pressed, the pin is disconnected (left 'floating').
A floating input has an undefined voltage and is susceptible to noise, causing erratic digital readings.
A pull-up resistor weakly holds the pin at VDD (logic high) when the button is open.
When the button is pressed, it overpowers the weak resistor and pulls the pin to GND (logic low).

questions.question: Compare and contrast the Push-Pull and Open-Drain output modes. Include in your answer a description of how each mode works, their typical applications, and a key advantage of each.
expectedPoints: Definition: Push-Pull uses two transistors (PMOS and NMOS) to actively drive the line high or low. Open-Drain uses only an NMOS to pull down or disconnect the line.
How it works: Push-Pull provides low impedance in both states. Open-Drain provides low impedance only when low; it is high-impedance (open) when 'high', relying on an external pull-up.
Applications: Push-Pull is general-purpose for LEDs, etc. Open-Drain is essential for multi-master buses (I²C) and 'wired-AND' configurations.
Advantage of Push-Pull: Strong drive, fast edges, no external components needed for basic operation.
Advantage of Open-Drain: Prevents bus contention, allows voltage level shifting, and enables multiple devices to share a line.
